comments:
Summary
The manuscript proposes a feature-based fuzzy rule–guided technique for image extraction/segmentation that combines outputs of up to 17 thresholding methods to build membership functions and a Mamdani fuzzy inference system. The authors claim improved results (MAE, MSE, PSNR) on noisy color images (example: 256x256 Baboon with Gaussian noise) compared to individual thresholding methods.

Strengths
- The idea of combining multiple thresholding outputs into a fuzzy rule framework is intuitively reasonable and could improve robustness compared to any single thresholding method.
- The paper situates itself in relevant prior work (thresholding, fuzzy segmentation, feature selection) and frames the motivation for fuzziness in segmentation.

Major weaknesses / concerns
1. Insufficient methodological detail
   - The core contribution—how membership functions and rules are constructed from the 17 threshold methods and from per-pixel feature vectors—is described only at a high level (list of steps) with no precise algorithms, parameters, or pseudocode. Key choices (shapes of membership functions, number and definition of fuzzy regions, rule generation algorithm, aggregation and defuzzification operators, how conflicts are resolved) are not specified.
   - The mapping from threshold outputs to ROIs and to fuzzy inputs/terms is vague. It is unclear whether fusion happens at feature or decision level in experiments and how the 17 methods were combined concretely.

2. Experimental evaluation is weak / under-reported
   - The text references tables and figures comparing PSNR values and shows qualitative examples, but the provided manuscript text does not include the quantitative tables, experimental settings, dataset descriptions, or the list of the 17 baseline thresholding algorithms used.
   - Only one example image (Baboon 256x256) is mentioned; no standard datasets, no train/test split, and no multiple-image evaluation to demonstrate generality.
   - No statistical analysis (e.g., variance across trials, significance) or runtime/complexity analysis is reported.
   - It is unclear whether comparisons use the same pre- and post-processing or parameter tuning; thus the claim of superiority is not well-supported.

3. Soundness and reproducibility
   - Because so many implementation details are omitted, the approach is not reproducible. Important hyperparameters (fuzzifier fh in the Gaussian membership, thresholds from each method, how features such as mean/std are computed per pixel or window size) are missing.
   - There is no ablation study showing the contribution of components (e.g., number of threshold methods used, choice of features, effect of membership shapes).

4. Presentation and clarity
   - The manuscript contains substantial repetition (paragraphs repeated near the end) and some typographical/formatting errors, which impede readability.
   - Figures and tables are referenced but not included in the provided text; descriptions do not provide enough specifics to evaluate claims.

Minor points
- The paper refers to prior work [15–17] extending grayscale FRBS to color images; it would help to clearly state differences to those works and what the new technical contribution is beyond applying existing ideas to color images.
- The choice of Mamdani system should be justified vis-à-vis other fuzzy or probabilistic fusion approaches.

Recommendations for improvement
1. Add a concrete algorithmic description: provide pseudocode for rule generation, membership-function design (including numeric parameters), rule evaluation and aggregation, and the decision-making process for final segmentation.
2. Report exhaustive experiments: include a list of the 17 thresholding methods used, datasets (multiple images and noise levels), quantitative results (tables) for MAE, MSE, PSNR across datasets and noise levels, and visual results. Provide baseline comparisons (each thresholding method, simple fusion strategies like majority vote and averaging, and other recent segmentation methods if possible).
3. Reproducibility: publish parameter settings, training/validation procedures (if any), and ideally provide code or pseudo-code sufficient for replication.
4. Ablation and sensitivity analyses: demonstrate how performance changes with number/type of threshold algorithms included, different membership shapes, and different fusion strategies.
5. Improve presentation: remove repeated text, fix typographical issues, and ensure all referenced figures/tables are present and legible.

Conclusion
The manuscript contains an idea that could be useful — combining multiple thresholding outputs via fuzzy rules — but in its current form it lacks the necessary methodological detail and rigorous experimental evidence to support the claims. I recommend substantial revision and expansion of experiments/documentation before the work can be judged as a solid contribution.
